role_summary: SNCC Mississippi project director from 1961; chief architect of the 1964 Freedom Summer; later founder of the Algebra Project (1982), which extended movement-organizing methods into mathematics education.

photo.alt: Bob Moses (right) with Julian Bond, "Fifty Years After Freedom Summer" event, February 2014
photo.photographer: Miller Center, University of Virginia (via Flickr)

ai_reading: The corpus discusses Moses across five transcripts: Timothy Jenkins (entry 120), Reginald Robinson (entry 101), Charles McLaurin (entry 17, the Sunflower County project director Moses oriented to voter-registration work), the John and Jean Rosenberg joint interview (entry 61), and Lawrence Guyot (entry 76, MFDP chairman). The cluster is the Mississippi-voter-registration-and-MFDP cohort, the network Moses built and ran across 1961-1964.
biographical_paragraph: Bob Moses is discussed in this corpus by 5 interviewees across the catalog (Timothy Jenkins (entry 120), Reginald Robinson (entry 101), Charles McLaurin (entry 17), John and Jean Rosenberg (entry 61), Lawrence Guyot (entry 76)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Bob Moses as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lawrence Guyot
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked by his interviewer to characterize Moses, MFDP chairman Lawrence Guyot describes the SNCC Mississippi project director he organized alongside:
interview_snippets.quote: Robert Moses is a young man who came to Mississippi, a mathematician in the philosopher, who was able to get the most out of people regardless of what they thought of themselves. Robert Moses was a person who was totally committed to social change, who was able to work with anyone.

interview_snippets.speaker: Charles McLaurin
interview_snippets.lead_in: Charles McLaurin recalls the August 1962 Jackson Freedom House meeting where he first laid eyes on Moses, having built up an outsized image of the man whose reputation preceded him:
interview_snippets.quote: And I'm looking all around because I haven't seen this fella that I'm expecting coming in. I've seen people coming in, you know. And he says, you got it Bob. And this little fella, coming out of the background out there. And Bob, Bob maybe about 5, 9, 5, 10, somewhere in there.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reginald Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: SNCC field secretary Reginald Robinson, sent to McComb to work with Moses in 1961, states the organizing principle Moses taught for accompanying registrants:
interview_snippets.quote: So that's when Bob Moses would say that we'd have to always stick with these people and walk with them wherever they go.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dorie Ann Ladner
interview_snippets.lead_in: In the joint interview with her sister Joyce, Dorie Ann Ladner recounts telling her mother she was leaving home to join the Mississippi movement:
interview_snippets.quote: I said, Mother, I packed a few little pieces and had a little small orange suitcase. I said, Mother, I'm going to go to Jackson and work with Bob Moses to get my freedom as I was going out the door to go downtown to catch the greyhound bus.

interview_snippets.speaker: Courtland Cox
interview_snippets.lead_in: At the August 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, Courtland Cox watched Moses decide whether to hand a congressman the list of Credentials Committee members backing the MFDP challenge:
interview_snippets.quote: Bob Moses just looked at him and stared. And I said to Bob, do you think he's going to steal the list? Bob hesitated a little more and then he gave him the list.

interview_snippets.speaker: Cleveland Sellers
interview_snippets.lead_in: Cleveland Sellers recalls the 1964 Freedom Summer orientation where Moses assembled the volunteers to tell them the first three field workers had gone missing in Mississippi:
interview_snippets.quote: And he said, I have you here because I want to let you know the three of our volunteers who were here yesterday have been missing. And that he had concluded as many of the other seasoned veterans that they were dead. And that many people sitting in the room had a choice, a decision to make.

sources.title: Robert P. Moses and Charles E. Cobb Jr., Radical Equations: Civil Rights from Mississippi to the Algebra Project
